engineGuide.h2: Mercedes-Benz M Class Engine Codes - Full Technical Specifications & Replacement Guide
engineGuide.h3: Every engine code fitted to the Mercedes-Benz M Class across all generations, with technical specs, compatible variants and trims, known failure points, and average rebuilt prices from UK specialists. Select any engine code to compare quotes.

entries.title: 2.7L Diesel
entries.history: Pioneered the 2.7L CDI era for the W163, delivering robust torque but later known for injector seal degradation.
compatibleVariants: ML 270 CDI, ML 270 CDI Elegance, ML 270 CDI Avantgarde
commonFailures: Injector copper seal leakage (60k+ miles)
EGR valve sticking and carbon buildup
Turbocharger actuator arm wear
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM647 Mercedes-Benz M Class engine replacement

entries.title: 3.2L Diesel
entries.history: Upgraded the W163 to a smoother 3.2L inline-six, offering refined highway cruising before the V6 diesel takeover.
compatibleVariants: ML 320 CDI, ML 320 CDI Elegance, ML 320 CDI Avantgarde, ML 320 CDI 4MATIC
commonFailures: EGR cooler gasket failure and coolant loss (~70k miles)
High-pressure injector pump calibration drift
Turbocharger shaft play at high mileage
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM648 Mercedes-Benz M Class engine replacement

entries.title: 3.0L Diesel
entries.history: Flagship 3.0L V6 diesel dominating W164 and W166 sales, renowned for efficiency but susceptible to oil cooler leaks.
compatibleVariants: ML 280 CDI, ML 300 CDI BlueEFFICIENCY, ML 350 CDI, ML 350 CDI 4MATIC, ML 350 CDI Sport, ML 350 CDI BlueTEC
commonFailures: Oil cooler and filter housing seal degradation (60k-80k miles)
EGR cooler internal cracking causing coolant contamination
Turbocharger VNT actuator electronic failure

entries.title: 4.0L Diesel
entries.history: Rare 4.0L V8 diesel for early W164 models, delivering massive torque but complex fuel systems raised upkeep costs.
compatibleVariants: ML 400 CDI, ML 420 CDI, ML 420 CDI 4MATIC, ML 420 CDI Grand Edition
commonFailures: High-pressure fuel pump internal wear (~80k miles)
Injector seal leakage and cylinder head carbon deposit buildup
Turbo wastegate actuator sticking
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM628 Mercedes-Benz M Class engine replacement

entries.title: 4.0L Diesel
entries.history: Twin-turbo V8 diesel for the ML 450 CDI, delivering exceptional mid-range pull and heavy-duty towing capability.
compatibleVariants: ML 450 CDI, ML 450 CDI 4MATIC, ML 450 CDI Grand Edition
commonFailures: Sequential turbocharger bearing wear (~90k miles)
Intercooler plastic pipe cracking under thermal stress
Turbo actuator electronic solenoid failure
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM629 Mercedes-Benz M Class engine replacement

entries.title: 3.2L Petrol
entries.history: Reliable early V6 establishing M Class smooth power, widely praised for straightforward maintenance and longevity.
compatibleVariants: ML 320, ML 320 Elegance, ML 320 Avantgarde, ML 320 4MATIC
commonFailures: Crankshaft harmonic balancer rubber separation (70k+ miles)
Valve cover gasket oil seepage onto wiring harnesses
Intake manifold coolant passage leak

compatibleVariants: ML 500, ML 500 4MATIC, ML 500 Elegance, ML 500 Avantgarde
commonFailures: Timing chain guide plastic degradation and stretch (80k+ miles)
Camshaft position sensor signal failure
Ignition coil pack breakdown causing cylinder misfire

commonProblems.h2: Mercedes-Benz M Class Engine Problems - What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense

problems.group: Mercedes-Benz M Class OM642 Oil Cooler Leak
problems.h4: Mercedes-Benz M Class OM642 Oil Cooler Leak - Why ML 350 CDI Engines Fail Prematurely
problems.affectedModels: Mercedes-Benz M Class ML 280 CDI, ML 300 CDI, ML 350 CDI (2005-2015, OM642.DE30.LA)
problems.rootCause: The OM642 oil cooler gaskets and plastic filter housing degrade under prolonged thermal cycling, causing high-pressure oil to seep into the cylinder head valley and eventually contaminate coolant or strip drive belts.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Draining coolant/oil, replacing revised Mercedes-spec gasket kit, updated plastic housing, and pressure testing the cooling circuit.
repairOptions.longevity: Solves early-stage seepage permanently when caught before cross-contamination. Not viable once oil enters the coolant system.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Full valley strip-down, chemical degreasing of cylinder head surfaces, cooler core replacement, and updated EGR cooler integration.
repairOptions.longevity: Restores factory sealing but carries residual risk if microscopic coolant ingress damaged head gasket surfaces.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit of a dyno-tested OM642 unit with reinforced cooler seals, upgraded tensioners, and bench-verified EGR calibration.
repairOptions.longevity: Eliminates legacy thermal weakness completely. Ideal for vehicles with prior overheating or hydrolock symptoms.

problems.recommendation: Address OM642 seepage at the first sign of dampness in the engine valley. If caught early, a specialist seal update with revised gaskets resolves the issue for under £1,000. Once oil migrates into the cooling system or timing covers, cross-contamination makes partial repair a false economy, and a full Mercedes-Benz M Class engine replacement becomes the only reliable path.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt OM642 engine prices for your Mercedes-Benz M Class

problems.group: Mercedes-Benz M Class M272 Balance Shaft Failure
problems.h4: Mercedes-Benz M Class M272 Balance Shaft Failure - Why ML 350 Petrol Engines Suffer Sudden Timing Collapse
problems.affectedModels: Mercedes-Benz M Class ML 350 Petrol (2005-2008, M272.9xx early series)
problems.rootCause: A manufacturing defect in the balance shaft idler gear causes premature tooth wear, sending metallic debris through the oil galleries, jamming the timing chain tensioner, and starving cam phasers of lubrication.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: No preventative tier exists once rattle or warning lights appear. Early replacement before symptoms is impractical on in-use vehicles.

repairOptions.tier: Major - Timing Assembly & Balance Shaft Update
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Removing timing covers, replacing balance shaft gear, updated tensioner, chain, and phasers, followed by extensive oil gallery flushing.
repairOptions.longevity: Technically viable only if debris hasn't reached the oil pump pickup. High labour risk of residual contamination in deep M272 galleries.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Swap with a specialist-rebuilt M272 block featuring hardened idler gear, reinforced oil pump, and zero-hour internal clearances.
repairOptions.longevity: Guarantees clean galleries, updated timing geometry, and eliminates catastrophic repeat risk. Recommended for any M272 showing chain rattle.

problems.recommendation: We advise skipping partial repairs on the M272 once audible rattle or cam timing codes appear. The balance shaft failure routinely drops debris past the main bearings, making in-car flushing unreliable. A full Mercedes-Benz M Class engine replacement with updated internal components delivers better long-term value and warranty security than attempting to salvage a contaminated short block.
problems.cta: Get quotes for M272 engine replacement on your Mercedes-Benz M Class

problems.group: Mercedes-Benz M Class Timing Chain Rattle
problems.h4: Mercedes-Benz M Class Timing Chain Rattle - Why ML 500 V8 Engines Stretch Over Time
problems.affectedModels: Mercedes-Benz M Class ML 500, ML 55 AMG (2005-2015, M273 / W163 M113)
problems.rootCause: Composite plastic timing chain guides become brittle from heat and oil exposure, allowing chain slack to increase. This stretches primary links, shifts cam timing, and can trigger piston-to-valve contact under load.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Front and rear chain cover removal, replacing plastic guides, hydraulic tensioners, and primary/secondary chains with OEM-spec replacements.
repairOptions.longevity: Effective if diagnosed before timing marks drift. Labour-intensive due to deep V8 sump design; high risk of overlooking rear rail wear.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit of a rebuilt M273/M113 unit with reinforced timing rails, upgraded tensioner calibration, and bench-tested oil pressure verification.
repairOptions.longevity: Eliminates guide degradation entirely. Delivers factory compression, fresh phasers, and zero-mile internal reliability for heavy-duty towing.

problems.recommendation: A preventative timing kit replacement is viable only if caught during routine service before cold-start rattle begins. However, given the ML 500's complex V8 layout, labour to access rear chains often exceeds the cost of a ready-to-fit unit. We recommend a full Mercedes-Benz M Class engine replacement to guarantee fresh tensioners, calibrated phasers, and a clean internal block without repeat labour charges.
problems.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz M Class ML 500 engine replacement costs today

problems.group: Mercedes-Benz M Class AMG Turbo Actuator Failure
problems.h4: Mercedes-Benz M Class AMG Turbo Actuator Failure - Why ML 63 Performance Engines Lose Power
problems.affectedModels: Mercedes-Benz M Class ML 63 AMG (2006-2015, M156 Naturally Aspirated / M157 Biturbo)
problems.rootCause: Extreme exhaust heat degrades turbocharger wastegate actuators and manifold gaskets, while high-RPM driving accelerates bucket shim wear and cam lobe scoring on the naturally aspirated M156 variant.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Removing intake plenum, replacing electronic actuator modules, calibrating VNT position, and updating ECU mapping parameters.
repairOptions.longevity: Restores limp mode quickly on M157 biturbo units. Does not address underlying manifold heat damage or bucket wear.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Removing cylinder heads for milling, installing reinforced valve springs, replacing turbocharger cores, and updating exhaust manifold gaskets.
repairOptions.longevity: Restores peak output but leaves block internals at original wear levels. High labour overlap makes it cost-prohibitive versus a full swap.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply & fit of a dyno-certified AMG-spec engine with upgraded valvetrain, reinforced turbo housings, and precision-balanced rotating assembly.
repairOptions.longevity: Delivers track-ready reliability, eliminates legacy heat fatigue, and preserves AMG performance characteristics under heavy throttle loads.

problems.recommendation: Actuator replacements solve limp mode temporarily but rarely address the systemic thermal stress that triggered the fault. For high-mileage ML 63 variants, a specialist-rebuilt engine with dyno-certified balancing, upgraded valve springs, and reinforced turbo housings delivers sustainable performance without the dealership premium.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt ML 63 AMG engine prices from UK specialists

finalCta.h4: Don't Let Engine Failure Write Off Your Mercedes-Benz M Class
finalCta.paragraph: Timing component fatigue, thermal seal degradation, and turbo actuator wear are well-documented across the Mercedes-Benz M Class range, but none of these issues should result in an unplanned write-off. EngineMarket connects you directly with accredited UK Mercedes-Benz M Class specialists who diagnose the exact failure mode, supply precision-rebuilt units with upgraded failure-prone components, and guarantee nationwide delivery. Every replacement engine includes a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty, rigorous compression testing, and full MOT compliance certification. Compare transparent quotes from vetted rebuilders and return to premium SUV reliability without the main dealer premium.
finalCta.buttonText: Compare Mercedes-Benz M Class Engine Replacement Prices Now →
finalCta.disclaimer: All prices are indicative UK market ranges based on historical quote data and repair requests from our network of engine specialists. Dealer pricing is estimated based on published main dealer labour rates and genuine OEM parts pricing. Specialist pricing reflects actual quote data from EngineMarket’s UK network. Actual costs vary depending on damage severity, vehicle condition, variant, and workshop location within the UK. Use our free comparison tool to get tailored quotes for your specific Mercedes-Benz M Class and engine code. Every engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty - always confirm exact terms with your chosen supplier.

engineSizes.h2: Mercedes-Benz M Class Engines by Size - Find the Right Displacement for Your Variant
engineSizes.intro: Every factory-fitted displacement across the Mercedes-Benz M Class range is listed below, complete with compatible engine codes, production years, and documented failure notes to help you identify the correct replacement unit.

groups.title: Mercedes-Benz M Class Diesel Engine Sizes

items.title: Mercedes-Benz M Class 2.7L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.7-litre inline-five established the entry point for the W163 generation, delivering reliable low-end torque for urban driving and light towing throughout its UK production run. Its common-rail architecture offered improved refinement over earlier indirect-injection units while maintaining robust mechanical durability for high-mileage ownership.
commonFailurePoints: turbocharger VNT actuator arm wear at higher mileage
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes-Benz M Class 2.7L diesel engines

items.title: Mercedes-Benz M Class 3.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 3.0-litre V6 became the definitive powerplant for the W164 and W166 generations, dominating the UK market through the highly popular ML 350 CDI and later BlueTEC variants. Featuring advanced turbocharging and intercooling, it balanced refined highway cruising with strong mid-range pull, though thermal management revisions were required across its lifespan.
commonFailurePoints: filter housing gasket degradation leading to coolant contamination
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes-Benz M Class 3.0L diesel engines

items.title: Mercedes-Benz M Class 3.2L Diesel Engines
items.description: Positioned as the flagship diesel for the late W163 generation, the 3.2-litre inline-six delivered smoother refinement and increased torque capacity for demanding towing applications. Its heavy-duty bottom end proved exceptionally durable, though early emissions hardware required periodic maintenance to sustain optimal performance.
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes-Benz M Class 3.2L diesel engines

items.title: Mercedes-Benz M Class 4.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The twin-turbocharged 4.0-litre V8 diesel represented the pinnacle of torque output for the W164 chassis, engineered for heavy-duty towing and rapid acceleration in the ML 420 and ML 450 CDI models. Despite its complex sequential turbo setup and high compression design, it delivers exceptional real-world efficiency when maintained with specialist-grade components.
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes-Benz M Class 4.0L diesel engines

groups.title: Mercedes-Benz M Class Petrol Engine Sizes

items.title: Mercedes-Benz M Class 3.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 3.2-litre V6 served as the standard petrol option for the inaugural W163 generation, offering smooth power delivery and straightforward mechanical architecture for early M Class adopters. Its naturally aspirated design prioritises long-term reliability, though aging cooling and ignition components require proactive inspection during high-mileage service intervals.
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz M Class 3.2L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz M Class 3.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: Evolving across the W164 and W166 generations, the 3.5-litre V6 transitioned from the early M272 unit to the direct-injection M276 architecture, improving both fuel economy and emissions compliance for modern UK driving. While the later direct-injection variant delivers sharper throttle response, the earlier models require attention to internal timing components to prevent catastrophic failure.
commonFailurePoints: Balance shaft idler gear wear on early M272 units
direct-injection intake valve carbon buildup on M276
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz M Class 3.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz M Class 3.7L Petrol Engines
items.description: Introduced as a mid-range upgrade for the W163 chassis, the 3.7-litre V6 provided increased displacement and improved torque for drivers seeking a more responsive petrol alternative to the smaller 3.2-litre unit. Its robust overhead-cam design remains highly sought after for classic M Class restorations due to its straightforward servicing requirements.
commonFailurePoints: Valve cover gasket oil seepage onto wiring harnesses
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz M Class 3.7L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz M Class 5.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 5.0-litre V8 anchored the luxury and performance hierarchy across multiple generations, evolving from the naturally aspirated M113 in the W163 to the twin-cam M273 in the W164. Delivering effortless high-speed cruising capability, this displacement combines premium refinement with substantial towing capacity when paired with the 4MATIC all-wheel-drive system.
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz M Class 5.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz M Class 5.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: Exclusive to the first-generation AMG performance variant, the supercharged 5.4-litre V8 combined robust M113 architecture with a Kompressor blower to deliver headline-grabbing acceleration for a premium SUV of its era. Its high-stress internal components require precise calibration and specialist rebuilding to sustain peak output under demanding driving conditions.
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz M Class 5.4L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz M Class 5.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: Bridging mainstream luxury and extreme performance, the 5.5-litre V8 powered both the refined ML 500 and the twin-turbocharged ML 63 AMG across the W164 and W166 platforms. The AMG-tuned variant utilises sequential turbochargers and forged internals to generate massive torque, while the standard road-going version focuses on quiet, effortless power delivery.
commonFailurePoints: Turbocharger electronic wastegate actuator failure on AMG variants
timing chain tensioner oil seal degradation
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz M Class 5.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz M Class 6.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 6.2-litre naturally aspirated V8 remains one of the most celebrated AMG powerplants, powering the W164 ML 63 AMG with explosive throttle response and a distinctive high-revving exhaust note. Engineered for sustained high-performance operation, its hand-assembled components require specialist machining and upgraded valve springs during any replacement procedure.
commonFailurePoints: Cylinder head bolt thread stretching under sustained thermal load
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz M Class 6.2L petrol engine prices

fuelTypes.h2: Mercedes-Benz M Class Engines by Fuel Type

items.title: Mercedes-Benz M Class Diesel Engines
items.description: Diesel CDI and BlueTEC units power the majority of UK M Class replacements, spanning the OM647 inline-five through to the dominant OM642 V6 across W163, W164 and W166 generations.
items.descriptor: Diesel CDI and BlueTEC units power the majority of UK M Class replacements, spanning the OM647 inline-five through to the dominant OM642 V6 across W163, W164 and W166 generations.
items.familiesLabel: Common Mercedes-Benz M Class Diesel Engine Families
families: OM642 - dominant 3.0L V6 (ML 350 CDI), oil cooler seal weakness per TSB LI01.40-P-059842
OM647 - reliable 2.7L inline-five (ML 270 CDI W163), injector seal wear at high mileage
OM648 - smooth 3.2L inline-six (ML 320 CDI W163), EGR cooler gasket failure common
OM629 - twin-turbo 4.0L V8 (ML 450 CDI), sequential turbo actuator complexity
OM628 - early 4.0L V8 diesel (ML 420 CDI), high-pressure pump calibration sensitivity
knownFor: Exceptional motorway fuel economy and towing capability across all generations
OM642 oil cooler/filter housing seal degradation at 60k-90k miles (high-volume repair)
Strong UK replacement demand on ML 350 CDI due to fleet prevalence and private ownership
DPF and AdBlue system compliance critical for post-2009 Euro 5/6 models
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes-Benz M Class Diesel replacement engines

items.title: Mercedes-Benz M Class Petrol Engines
items.description: Petrol V6 and V8 units deliver refined performance across all M Class generations, from the robust M112/M113 classics to the direct-injection M276 and high-output AMG M156/M157 powerplants.
items.descriptor: Petrol V6 and V8 units deliver refined performance across all M Class generations, from the robust M112/M113 classics to the direct-injection M276 and high-output AMG M156/M157 powerplants.
items.familiesLabel: Common Mercedes-Benz M Class Petrol Engine Families
families: M272 - 3.5L V6 (ML 350 W164), balance shaft idler gear failure on early pre-2008 units
M276 - 3.5L direct-injection V6 (ML 350 W166), improved efficiency with intake carbon buildup risk
M273 - 5.5L V8 (ML 500 W164), timing chain guide wear and cam adjuster magnet degradation
M112/M113 - classic 3.2L-5.4L V6/V8 (W163), harmonic balancer and timing guide aging issues
M156/M157 - AMG 6.2L NA / 5.5L biturbo V8 (ML 63), valve train and turbo actuator specialist rebuilds
foundIn: W166 (2011-2019) - M276 3.5L, M278 4.6L biturbo, M157 5.5L biturbo (ML 63 AMG)
knownFor: Smooth, refined power delivery with strong mid-range torque across V6 and V8 configurations
M272 balance shaft catastrophic failure risk on early W164 ML 350 petrol (TSB LI01.20-P-058990)
M273/M113 timing chain guide plastic degradation requiring preventative inspection at 80k+ miles
High specialist demand for AMG M156/M157 rebuilds with dyno certification and upgraded valvetrain
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz M Class Petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes-Benz M Class Hybrid / Electrified Engines
items.description: The Mercedes-Benz M Class was not offered with hybrid, plug-in hybrid, mild-hybrid or electric powertrains in the UK market during its 1997-2019 production run.
items.descriptor: The Mercedes-Benz M Class was not offered with hybrid, plug-in hybrid, mild-hybrid or electric powertrains in the UK market during its 1997-2019 production run.
items.familiesLabel: Mercedes-Benz M Class Electrified Engine Codes
families: None - no factory-fitted hybrid or electric variants were sold in the UK for this model
All UK M Class models use conventional combustion engines with automatic transmission and 4MATIC AWD option
No battery-electric drive units, starter-generators or plug-in charging systems to maintain or replace
Replacement focus remains on OM642 diesel and M272/M276 petrol engine families exclusively
foundIn: Not applicable - no electrified M Class variants in UK specification
importantNotes: If your M Class has a 48-volt or hybrid badge, verify via registration - it may be a later GLE rebrand (post-2019)
Standard diesel/petrol engine replacement procedures apply to all UK M Class models
Specialist suppliers focus on OM642, M272, M276, M156 and M157 codes for this generation

engineYears.h2: Mercedes-Benz M Class Engines by Year - What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: This technical timeline covers every major engine change across the Mercedes-Benz M Class production run (1997-2019), organised so owners can quickly identify their engine code, generation and compare rebuilt engine prices from UK specialists.

years.preview: W163 Launch: First Generation M Class
years.description: The inaugural W163 M Class introduced Mercedes-Benz to the premium SUV segment with robust inline-five and V6 petrol/diesel powertrains designed for durability and towing capability.
keyChanges: Launch of Mercedes-Benz first dedicated SUV platform with permanent 4MATIC all-wheel drive option
Introduction of OM647 2.7L inline-five diesel and M112 3.2L V6 petrol as core powertrains
ML 55 AMG variant launched with supercharged M113K 5.4L V8 for performance flagship positioning
knownFor: Robust mechanical architecture with straightforward servicing requirements for classic SUV restoration
OM647 injector copper seal degradation at 70k+ miles requiring specialist copper washer replacement
M113 timing chain guide plastic becoming brittle with age - inspect before high-mileage purchase
Strong enthusiast community support for W163 parts availability and technical documentation
years.cta: Get quotes for 1997-2001 Mercedes-Benz M Class replacement engines
years.ticker: ML 270 CDI engine replacement • W163 OM647 injector seal fix • ML 55 AMG supercharger rebuild specialist

years.preview: W163 Maturity: OM648 Inline-Six Diesel Introduced
years.description: The final W163 production years refined the platform with the smoother OM648 3.2L inline-six diesel and updated emissions hardware ahead of the W164 transition.
keyChanges: Introduction of OM648 3.2L inline-six diesel replacing OM647 for improved refinement and torque
Updated common-rail injection calibration across diesel range for Euro 3 compliance
Final W163 production with enhanced interior trim and chassis tuning before W164 launch
knownFor: OM648 inline-six delivers exceptional smoothness but EGR cooler gasket failure common at 80k+ miles
Late W163 models command premium for classic SUV restoration due to mechanical simplicity
M112 harmonic balancer rubber separation requires inspection before high-mileage purchase
Strong towing capability with robust 5-speed automatic transmission pairing
years.cta: Compare 2002-2005 Mercedes-Benz M Class engine prices

years.preview: W164 Launch: New Generation, New Engine Families
years.description: The all-new W164 platform introduced the dominant OM642 3.0L V6 diesel and M272 3.5L V6 petrol, establishing the powertrain architecture for the next decade of M Class production.
keyChanges: Complete platform redesign with improved chassis dynamics, interior quality and safety systems
Introduction of OM642 3.0L V6 diesel replacing inline-five/six units for compact packaging and efficiency
M272 3.5L V6 petrol launched with variable valve timing, replacing M112 architecture
ML 420 CDI introduced with OM628 4.0L V8 diesel for heavy-duty towing applications
mainEngines: OM642.DE30.LA - 3.0L V6 diesel (ML 280 CDI, ML 350 CDI)
knownFor: OM642 becomes the definitive M Class diesel - powers ~75% of UK fleet replacements today
Early M272 units (pre-2008) carry balance shaft idler gear failure risk per TSB LI01.20-P-058990
OM642 oil cooler/filter housing seal degradation emerges as high-volume failure at 60k-90k miles
W164 chassis refinement significantly improves ride quality and NVH over W163 predecessor
years.cta: Get quotes for 2005-2006 Mercedes-Benz M Class replacement engines
years.ticker: OM642 oil cooler leak repair • M272 balance shaft failure fix • ML 350 CDI engine replacement specialist

years.preview: W164 Euro 5 Transition: Emissions Hardware Updates
years.description: Mid-cycle W164 production introduced Euro 5 emissions compliance hardware, revised engine calibrations and the high-output OM629 twin-turbo V8 diesel for performance variants.
keyChanges: Euro 5 emissions compliance introduced with revised EGR, DPF and calibration strategies
OM629 4.0L twin-turbo V8 diesel launched for ML 450 CDI performance variant
ML 63 AMG introduced with naturally aspirated M156 6.2L V8 replacing supercharged M113K
Updated engine management software across OM642 and M272 families for improved drivability
mainEngines: OM642.9xx - 3.0L V6 diesel Euro 5 (ML 300 CDI, ML 350 CDI)
knownFor: OM642 Euro 5 calibration requires precise DPF/AdBlue system matching for MOT compliance
M156 AMG valve train bucket wear emerges as high-mileage concern on early ML 63 units
OM629 sequential turbocharger actuator complexity increases specialist rebuild requirements
Euro 5 OM642 units benefit from revised oil cooler seals but remain susceptible to thermal degradation
years.cta: Compare 2007-2009 Mercedes-Benz M Class engine prices
years.ticker: OM642 Euro 5 DPF compatibility • ML 63 AMG M156 valve train rebuild • OM629 turbo actuator replacement
enquiries: ML 63 AMG M156 valve train rebuild

years.description: The facelifted W164 received updated interior technology, revised suspension tuning and final engine calibrations before the W166 generation launch.
keyChanges: Exterior and interior facelift with updated lighting, infotainment and driver assistance systems
Final OM642 calibration updates for improved fuel economy and emissions stability
M272 replaced by early M276 direct-injection architecture in select markets ahead of W166
Final production of OM628/OM629 V8 diesel variants before W166 rationalisation
mainEngines: OM642.9xx - 3.0L V6 diesel facelift calibration (ML 350 CDI)
M276.DEH35.LA - 3.5L direct-injection V6 petrol (early ML 350 petrol, select markets)
M156.E62 - 6.2L V8 petrol (ML 63 AMG, final W164 production)
knownFor: Late W164 OM642 units benefit from revised seal materials but remain susceptible to thermal cycling degradation
M276 direct-injection introduction brings improved efficiency but requires intake valve carbon management
Facelift W164 models command premium for used market due to enhanced equipment and refinement
Final M156 W164 units represent collectible naturally aspirated AMG performance before turbo era
years.cta: Get quotes for 2010-2011 Mercedes-Benz M Class replacement engines

years.preview: W166 Launch: Third Generation, Refined Powertrains
years.description: The all-new W166 platform introduced lightweight construction, advanced driver assistance and refined OM642/M276 powertrains with improved efficiency and emissions performance.
keyChanges: Complete platform redesign with aluminium-intensive body, improved aerodynamics and enhanced safety
OM642 retained but with revised calibration, improved cooling and Euro 5b+ compliance
M276 direct-injection V6 petrol becomes standard, replacing M272 across petrol range
Introduction of 7G-TRONIC PLUS automatic transmission across all variants for improved efficiency
mainEngines: OM642.DE30.LA - 3.0L V6 diesel Euro 5b+ (ML 250 BlueTEC, ML 350 CDI)
knownFor: W166 OM642 benefits from revised oil cooler design but seal degradation remains a known failure point
M276 direct-injection requires periodic intake valve decarbonisation for optimal long-term performance
M157 twin-turbo AMG delivers exceptional performance but turbo actuator electronic failures emerge at 70k+ miles
Enhanced chassis refinement and NVH isolation make W166 the most comfortable M Class generation
years.cta: Compare 2011-2013 Mercedes-Benz M Class engine prices
years.ticker: W166 OM642 oil leak fix • M276 intake carbon cleaning • ML 63 AMG M157 turbo replacement

years.preview: W166 Euro 6 / BlueTEC: Advanced Emissions Compliance
years.description: Mid-cycle W166 production introduced mandatory Euro 6 compliance with AdBlue SCR systems, revised engine management and enhanced emissions durability across the diesel range.
keyChanges: Euro 6 emissions compliance mandatory with AdBlue SCR dosing systems across all diesel variants
Revised OM642 calibration with enhanced thermal management and DPF regeneration strategies
Introduction of 9G-TRONIC nine-speed automatic transmission for improved efficiency and refinement
Updated infotainment, driver assistance and connectivity features across the range
mainEngines: OM642.DE30.LA Euro 6 - 3.0L V6 diesel with AdBlue (ML 350 BlueTEC)
M276.DEH35.LA - 3.5L direct-injection V6 petrol (ML 350 petrol, continued)
M157.DEH55 - 5.5L twin-turbo V8 petrol (ML 63 AMG, continued)
knownFor: Euro 6 OM642 requires precise AdBlue dosing module calibration for MOT emissions compliance
M276 high-pressure fuel pump internal wear emerges as known failure point at 60k+ miles
M157 AMG turbocharger electronic wastegate actuator failures common at 70k-90k miles
W166 Euro 6 models represent the most technologically advanced M Class with comprehensive driver assistance
years.cta: Get quotes for 2014-2017 Mercedes-Benz M Class replacement engines
years.ticker: OM642 AdBlue dosing module replacement • M276 HPFP failure fix • ML 63 AMG turbo actuator calibration

years.preview: W166 Final Production: End of M Class Era
years.description: The final M Class production years received minor equipment updates before the model was rebranded as GLE for the next generation, marking the end of the iconic M Class nameplate.
keyChanges: Final W166 production with minor equipment updates and special edition variants
Model rebranded as Mercedes-Benz GLE for subsequent generation (post-2019)
Final OM642 and M276 calibration updates for emissions durability and drivability refinement
End of production for naturally aspirated AMG variants before full turbocharged portfolio
mainEngines: OM642.DE30.LA Euro 6 Final - 3.0L V6 diesel with AdBlue (ML 350 BlueTEC final)
M276.DEH35.LA Final - 3.5L direct-injection V6 petrol (ML 350 petrol final)
M278.DEH46.LA Final - 4.6L twin-turbo V8 petrol (ML 500 final)
M157.DEH55 Final - 5.5L twin-turbo V8 petrol (ML 63 AMG final)
knownFor: Final W166 models represent the most refined and technologically advanced M Class specification
Late-production OM642 units benefit from cumulative seal and calibration improvements but remain susceptible to thermal degradation
M157 final units represent the pinnacle of W166 AMG performance before GLE transition
Strong collector interest in final M Class badged vehicles as the nameplate transitions to GLE

faq.h2: Mercedes-Benz M Class Engine Replacement - Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: How much does a Mercedes-Benz M Class engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A rebuilt Mercedes-Benz M Class engine typically costs £2,400-£4,800 supply-only across the full model range, with OM642 diesel and M272 petrol variants dominating this pricing bracket. Adding specialist supply & fit service increases the total investment by £450-£850 depending on location and variant complexity, while AMG powertrains command premium pricing due to specialist rebuild requirements.
keyPoints: OM642 3.0L diesel rebuilds average £2,400-£3,800 for ML 350 CDI variants
M272 3.5L petrol replacements range £2,500-£4,000 for early W164 models
AMG M156/M157 engines cost £4,500-£9,500 due to specialist rebuild complexity
Used engines start from £1,200 but require compression testing before purchase
Euro 5/6 OM642 units may require AdBlue calibration adding £200-£400
items.cta: Compare Mercedes-Benz M Class engine replacement prices from UK specialists today

items.question: What is the most reliable Mercedes-Benz M Class engine?
items.answer: The OM647 2.7L inline-five diesel in early W163 ML 270 CDI models proves most reliable, with robust mechanical design and fewer complex emissions components. Later OM642 3.0L V6 units are dependable when oil cooler seals are proactively replaced around 60k-80k miles per known Mercedes-Benz technical service guidance.
keyPoints: OM647 2.7L inline-five diesel offers proven longevity with straightforward servicing requirements
OM642 3.0L V6 reliability improves significantly after oil cooler seal TSB update
M112/M113 petrol V6/V8 units are robust but require timing guide inspection
Avoid early M272 3.5L petrol engines pre-2008 due to balance shaft risk
Regular oil changes with MB 229.51 spec oil extends all diesel engine life
items.cta: Find a reconditioned Mercedes-Benz M Class engine with 12-month warranty coverage

items.question: What causes OM642 oil leaks in the Mercedes-Benz M Class ML 350 CDI?
items.answer: OM642 oil leaks in the Mercedes-Benz M Class ML 350 CDI typically stem from degraded oil cooler gaskets and plastic filter housing seals failing under thermal cycling. This known issue affects most units between 60k-90k miles and can contaminate coolant if ignored, requiring prompt specialist attention.
keyPoints: Oil cooler gasket degradation is the primary cause of OM642 seepage issues
Plastic filter housing cracks under repeated heat cycles in W164/W166 models
Revised seal kit A6421800003 resolves the issue when fitted by specialists
Early detection prevents costly cross-contamination between oil and cooling systems
Mercedes-Benz TSB LI01.40-P-059842 documents the official repair procedure
items.cta: Get quotes for OM642 Mercedes-Benz M Class engine replacement with updated seals

items.question: Is the M272 balance shaft failure common in Mercedes-Benz M Class ML 350 petrol models?
items.answer: Yes, early M272 3.5L petrol engines in Mercedes-Benz M Class ML 350 models built before 2008 suffer balance shaft idler gear wear, a known catastrophic fault. This issue affects engines between 50k-80k miles and requires complete engine replacement once metallic debris contaminates oil galleries.
keyPoints: Balance shaft idler gear defect affects M272 engines built before 2008 model year
Audible timing rattle on cold start signals imminent catastrophic failure risk
Mercedes-Benz TSB LI01.20-P-058990 confirms the defect and updated part number
Replacement with rebuilt M272 unit featuring hardened gear is the only reliable fix
Post-2008 M272 and M276 direct-injection variants do not share this fault
items.cta: Get quotes for M272 Mercedes-Benz M Class engine replacement with updated components

items.question: Should I replace or repair my Mercedes-Benz M Class engine?
items.answer: For Mercedes-Benz M Class engines with catastrophic internal damage like balance shaft failure or hydrolock, full replacement offers better long-term value than attempting in-car repairs. Minor issues like injector seals or turbo actuators may justify targeted repair if diagnosed early by a Mercedes-Benz M Class specialist.
keyPoints: Full replacement guarantees clean oil galleries and zero-hour internal reliability
Targeted repairs work only for isolated faults before cross-contamination occurs
Rebuilt engines include upgraded components addressing known Mercedes-Benz M Class failure points
Supply & fit packages from specialists ensure warranty validity and MOT compliance
Compare quotes before deciding - replacement often costs less than major in-car repairs
items.cta: Compare rebuilt versus repair costs for your Mercedes-Benz M Class engine today

items.question: What is the lifespan of a Mercedes-Benz M Class diesel engine?
items.answer: A well-maintained Mercedes-Benz M Class diesel engine like the OM642 or OM647 can reliably reach 150k-200k miles with regular servicing using MB 229.51 spec oil. Most replacement enquiries occur between 60k-90k miles due to known seal degradation rather than fundamental engine wear.
keyPoints: OM647 2.7L inline-five often exceeds 200k miles with proactive maintenance schedules
OM642 3.0L V6 lifespan depends heavily on oil cooler seal replacement timing
DPF and AdBlue systems on Euro 5/6 units require specialist calibration for longevity
High-mileage examples benefit from compression testing before purchasing used replacements
Regular oil changes every 10k miles significantly extend all Mercedes-Benz M Class diesel life
items.cta: Find a low-mileage tested Mercedes-Benz M Class diesel engine with warranty

items.question: Can I fit a used Mercedes-Benz M Class engine myself?
items.answer: While technically possible, fitting a used Mercedes-Benz M Class engine yourself risks voiding warranty, MOT failure, and costly errors on complex emissions systems. We strongly recommend accredited Mercedes-Benz M Class specialists who ensure calibration coding, emissions compliance, and warranty validity for your peace of mind.
keyPoints: OM642 and M272 engines require ECU coding after installation for proper operation
Specialist tools are needed for timing component alignment on V6 and V8 units
Incorrect installation can damage turbochargers, DPF systems, or AdBlue dosing modules
Accredited fitters provide warranty-backed work that independent DIY cannot match
Supply & fit packages often cost less than sourcing tools and risking errors
items.cta: Find an accredited Mercedes-Benz M Class engine supply and fit specialist near you

items.question: Will a reconditioned Mercedes-Benz M Class engine pass MOT emissions testing?
items.answer: Yes, a properly reconditioned Mercedes-Benz M Class engine from EngineMarket specialists will pass MOT emissions testing when fitted by an accredited technician. All rebuilt units include updated seals, calibrated components, and documentation confirming Euro 4/5/6 compliance for your specific Mercedes-Benz M Class variant and model year.
keyPoints: Reconditioned OM642 units include revised oil cooler seals preventing leak-related failures
Emissions compliance documentation is provided with every rebuilt Mercedes-Benz M Class engine
Specialist fitting ensures DPF, EGR and AdBlue systems function within MOT limits
12-month unlimited mileage warranty covers emissions-related component failures post-installation
Pre-MOT compression and emissions testing available from our accredited specialist network
items.cta: Get a reconditioned Mercedes-Benz M Class engine with MOT compliance certification

items.question: What's the difference between reconditioned and rebuilt Mercedes-Benz M Class engines?
items.answer: Reconditioned Mercedes-Benz M Class engines undergo partial disassembly with worn seals and gaskets replaced, while rebuilt units feature machined components, upgraded failure-prone parts, and dyno testing. Rebuilt engines cost more but offer superior longevity for high-mileage Mercedes-Benz M Class variants or known failure patterns like M272 balance shaft issues.
keyPoints: Reconditioned units replace seals, gaskets and bearings during partial engine strip-down
Rebuilt engines include machined components and upgraded parts addressing known faults
Dyno testing on rebuilt units verifies compression and oil pressure before dispatch
Both options include minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty from UK specialists
Choose rebuilt for catastrophic failures, reconditioned for preventative maintenance scenarios
items.cta: Compare reconditioned versus rebuilt Mercedes-Benz M Class engine prices and warranties

items.question: How do I know if my Mercedes-Benz M Class needs an engine replacement?
items.answer: Key signs your Mercedes-Benz M Class needs engine replacement include persistent knocking noises, blue smoke from exhaust, coolant-oil mixing, or loss of compression on multiple cylinders. Diagnostic testing by a Mercedes-Benz M Class specialist confirms whether targeted repair or full replacement offers the most cost-effective solution for your variant.
keyPoints: Metallic knocking on cold start often signals M272 balance shaft or timing failure
Blue exhaust smoke indicates piston ring or valve seal wear requiring assessment
Milky oil or coolant contamination suggests head gasket or oil cooler failure
Compression testing across all cylinders identifies whether repair or replacement is viable
Specialist diagnosis prevents unnecessary replacement costs on minor Mercedes-Benz M Class faults
items.cta: Get a specialist diagnosis for your Mercedes-Benz M Class engine concerns today
